3.happening: Measure the grid — how many rows it has.
3.why: We need the bounds to scan every cell and test neighbors.
3.invariant: The grid's dimensions are fixed for the whole run.

4.happening: Measure how many columns each row has.
4.why: Column count bounds our left/right moves.
4.invariant: Every row has the same width.

5.why: It will hold rotten oranges whose neighbors still need to rot.
5.invariant: Every rotten orange is processed exactly once.

6.why: We must know how many fresh oranges exist to detect leftovers.
6.invariant: fresh equals the number of fresh oranges not yet rotted.

7.happening: Loop over each row to inspect its cells.
7.why: We must find every rotten orange (a BFS source) and count fresh ones.
7.invariant: Rows above r are fully inspected.

8.happening: Loop over each column in row {r}.
8.why: We inspect each cell exactly once.
8.invariant: Cells left of c in this row are done.

9.why: Rotten oranges are the starting points of the spread.
9.invariant: Every rotten orange becomes a BFS source at minute 0.

10.happening: Seed the queue with rotten orange ({r},{c}) at minute 0.
10.why: The spread begins from all rotten oranges simultaneously.

11.why: Fresh oranges are the targets that must rot.
11.invariant: Each fresh orange is counted once.

12.why: We track how many still need to rot.
12.invariant: fresh rises by one for each fresh orange found.

13.invariant: minutes is the latest rot-time seen so far.

14.happening: List the four orthogonal moves (up, down, right, left).
14.why: Rotting spreads only horizontally or vertically.
14.invariant: Only these four directions are ever tried.

15.happening: Set the queue's front marker head to 0.
15.why: It points at the next orange to process.
15.invariant: Oranges before head are already processed.

16.happening: Reset the current-row marker to -1 (no active orange).
16.why: There's an active orange only while spreading.

17.invariant: cur_r and cur_c together locate the active orange.

18.happening: The queue still holds {len(queue)} rotten orange(s) — keep spreading.
18.why: Each queued orange can still rot its neighbors.
18.invariant: head advances toward the back as oranges are processed.

18.happening: The queue is drained — the rot has spread as far as it can.
18.why: No rotten orange has any unprocessed neighbor left.
18.invariant: Every reachable fresh orange has rotted.

19.happening: Look at the orange at the front of the queue.
19.why: BFS processes the earliest-rotted orange next.

20.happening: Advance the front marker (dequeue this orange).
20.why: This orange is now spreading, so move on to the next.

21.why: We need its coordinates to reach neighbors.
21.invariant: cell holds a [row, col, minute] triple.

22.why: Row and column locate the active orange.
22.invariant: cur_r, cur_c now point at the spreading orange.

23.happening: Read the minute at which this orange rotted.
23.why: Its neighbors will rot one minute later.
23.invariant: t is when this orange became rotten.

24.happening: The answer is the latest rot-minute seen so far.
24.why: The elapsed time is when the last orange rots.

25.happening: Try each of the four directions from this orange.
25.why: To find fresh oranges it can rot.
25.invariant: All four neighbors are checked before moving on.

26.why: The direction's row offset added to the active orange.
26.invariant: nr may be off-grid — we check next.

27.why: The direction's column offset added to the active orange.
27.invariant: nc may be off-grid — we check next.

28.when: nr >= 0 && nr < rows && nc >= 0 && nc < cols && grid[nr][nc] == 1
28.happening: Neighbor ({nr},{nc}) is a fresh orange — it's about to rot.
28.why: A fresh orange next to a rotten one rots this minute.
28.invariant: Only in-bounds fresh oranges get infected.

28.happening: Neighbor ({nr},{nc}) is off-grid, empty, or already rotten — skip it.
28.invariant: Empty cells and rotten oranges are never re-queued.

29.happening: Rot the fresh orange at ({nr},{nc}) — set it to 2.
29.why: It has just been infected by its rotten neighbor.
29.invariant: A cell rots at most once.

30.why: We track the count to detect any that can never rot.
30.invariant: fresh drops by one each time an orange rots.

31.happening: Queue the newly-rotten orange for minute {t}+1.
31.why: Next minute it will rot its own fresh neighbors.
31.invariant: Each orange enters the queue exactly once.

32.happening: {fresh} fresh orange(s) never rotted — they were unreachable.
32.why: Some fresh orange has no path from any rotten one.
32.invariant: An unreachable fresh orange makes the task impossible.

32.happening: No fresh oranges remain — every one rotted.
32.why: The spread reached the entire grid of fresh oranges.
32.invariant: The elapsed time is a valid answer.

33.happening: Return -1 — not all oranges can rot.
33.why: At least one fresh orange is unreachable.

34.why: Every orange rotted; this is when the last one did.

init.happening: Setting up: seeding the queue with all rotten oranges and counting fresh ones.
init.why: Multi-source BFS starts from every rotten orange at once.

loop.happening: Spreading the rot outward from a rotten orange.
loop.why: Each rotten orange infects its fresh 4-neighbors.
loop.invariant: Oranges rot in waves of increasing minutes.

check.happening: Deciding whether a neighbor can rot, or whether any fresh orange survived.
check.why: Only in-bounds fresh oranges rot; leftovers mean -1.
check.invariant: Each fresh orange rots at most once.

update.happening: Recording the spread — rotting an orange and queuing it for next minute.
update.why: State must reflect which oranges have rotted and when.
update.invariant: fresh drops as oranges rot; minutes only grows.

move.happening: Advancing the wavefront — dequeuing an orange and reaching toward neighbors.
move.why: Breadth-first spreads the rot minute by minute.

return.happening: Spread complete — reporting the elapsed minutes (or -1).
return.why: Either all oranges rotted, or some were unreachable.
